name: Harut
description: Harut is an angel mentioned in Islamic tradition, specifically in the Quran (2:102). He is often associated with Marut, forming a pair of angels whose story revolves around a divine test of humanity. According to some interpretations, these angels, critical of human fallibility, were challenged by God to demonstrate their own righteousness when subjected to earthly temptations. They were subsequently sent to Earth and succumbed to human desires, including intoxication and illicit relationships, thereby proving humanity's inherent weakness and the necessity of divine guidance. Their story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of hubris and the seductive power of temptation. The angels are often associated with teaching humans magic, although the nature and purpose of this magic is subject to varied interpretations, ranging from legitimate knowledge to harmful sorcery. Their narrative highlights complex theological themes concerning free will, divine judgment, and the inherent flaws of both angelic and human beings.
subtitle: The Fallen Angel

keyMyths.title: The Descent to Earth
keyMyths.description: Harut and Marut, critical of human behavior, were challenged by God to live as humans and resist temptation. They descended to Earth, but quickly succumbed to earthly desires. They fell in love with a beautiful woman named Zohra (often identified with Venus), who demanded they reveal the secret to ascending back to heaven. This act of revealing divine secrets cemented their fall and resulted in their punishment.

keyMyths.title: The Teaching of Magic
keyMyths.description: While on Earth, Harut and Marut are said to have taught humans magic. Some interpretations suggest this was done to test humanity, distinguishing between good and evil. Others portray it as a consequence of their fall, providing humans with knowledge that could be used for harmful purposes. This teaching is often linked to the separation of a man and his wife, a common element in stories of black magic.
keyMyths.source: Ibn Kathir, *Tafsir al-Qur'an al-'Azim* (Commentary on the Great Quran)

keyMyths.title: The Punishment
keyMyths.description: As punishment for their transgressions, Harut and Marut were given a choice: endure worldly torment or eternal damnation. They chose earthly suffering and are said to be suspended upside down in a well in Babylon, where they remain as a warning to others. They are chained and will remain so until the Day of Judgment, a constant reminder of the consequences of disobedience to God's will.

sources.author: Wheeler, Brannon M.
sources.work: *Prophets in the Quran: An Introduction to the Quran for Western Readers*
sources.description: Provides context for understanding Quranic narratives, including the story of Harut and Marut.

associations.name: Temptation
associations.significance: Harut and Marut's story is intrinsically linked to the concept of resisting worldly temptations and the consequences of succumbing to them.

associations.name: Divine Judgement
associations.significance: The angels' actions and subsequent punishment reflect the divine judgement and accountability that applies to all beings, regardless of their status.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: The pair of Harut and Marut emphasizes duality and the balance between good and evil, or righteousness and fallibility.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Some accounts link Harut and Marut to fallen stars, symbolizing their descent from a higher spiritual plane to the earthly realm.

associations.name: Free Will
associations.significance: The narrative explores the concept of free will and the choices that individuals make, highlighting the potential for both virtue and vice.

associations.name: Repentance
associations.significance: The angels' punishment offers a path to repentance and seeking forgiveness for their transgressions, a key theme in Abrahamic religions.

cultural.modernLegacy: Harut and Marut continue to be figures of discussion and interpretation within Islamic theology and Sufi mysticism. Their story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of arrogance and the importance of humility. They also appear in some forms of Islamic art and literature.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Harut and Marut are rooted in Islamic tradition, primarily referenced in the Quran (2:102). While the Quran does not provide exhaustive details, it mentions that they were sent down to Babylon. Interpretations vary, but a common understanding is that they were angels who questioned God's creation of humanity, citing human fallibility and propensity for sin. God, in turn, challenged them to live on Earth and resist worldly temptations. Some accounts suggest that they were not initially named Harut and Marut but were given these names after their descent. The pre-Islamic sources and folklore surrounding these figures are difficult to verify, but the Quranic verse remains the central source for their story and subsequent interpretations within Islamic scholarship. The narrative serves as a theological exploration of divine wisdom and human frailty.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth surrounding Harut and Marut involves their descent to Earth and subsequent fall from grace. According to popular interpretations, after criticizing humanity, God challenged them to live as humans and resist temptation. They descended to Babylon, where they were granted human desires. They were warned against idolatry, murder, and fornication, but they eventually succumbed to these temptations. One version of the story involves a woman named Zohra (often identified with Venus), whom they both desired. To win her favor, they committed acts against God's will. As a result of their disobedience, they were punished. Some accounts state they were given a choice between earthly punishment and eternal suffering in the afterlife, and they chose earthly punishment. They are now suspended upside down in a well in Babylon, where they remain until the Day of Judgment, teaching magic as a test for humanity.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The story of Harut and Marut has had a lasting impact on Islamic thought and culture. It serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of arrogance and the importance of humility, even for those in positions of perceived spiritual superiority. Their story is often cited in discussions about free will, temptation, and the nature of divine justice. Within Sufi mysticism, Harut and Marut are sometimes seen as symbols of the struggle against the lower self (nafs) and the need for constant vigilance against temptation. Their tale also highlights the complexities of divine testing and the potential for even the most righteous beings to fall from grace. Their story continues to be retold and reinterpreted in various forms of Islamic literature and art, serving as a reminder of the constant struggle between good and evil.

shortDescription: Angels in Islamic tradition who descended to Earth and succumbed to temptation, serving as a divine test and cautionary tale.
